name: How to Use Safety Data Sheets (SDS) to Win Your Chemical Exposure Claim

step.name: Step 1
step.text:
Chemical exposure claims are unique because the 'accident' isn't always a single event. It can be the slow accumulation of toxins in your lungs, skin, or blood over years of service. Proving these cases requires a systematic approach to evidence and medical documentation.

### Step 1: Document Your Symptoms and Environment
As soon as you notice respiratory issues, skin rashes, or neurological symptoms, start a log. Note when the symptoms are worst. Do they improve over the weekend when you are away from work? This 'de-challenge and re-challenge' observation is powerful evidence.

### Step 2: Request the Safety Data Sheets (SDS)
Under OSHA regulations, your employer must provide you with the SDS for every chemical in your work area. These documents detail the known health hazards. If you are breathing 'fumes,' the SDS will tell you what those fumes are and what damage they do to the human body. Take these sheets to your doctor.

### Step 3: Get a Specialized Medical Opinion
Your family doctor might not be familiar with occupational toxicology. You may need to see a specialist—like a pulmonologist for lung issues or a dermatologist for skin conditions—who has experience in industrial exposures. They must be willing to state that your condition is 'more likely than not' caused by your workplace environment.

### Step 4: Report the Occupational Disease
Once a doctor makes the connection between your illness and your work, you must report it to your employer. In Utah and Idaho, the clock for the statute of limitations starts when you became aware of the link. 

### Step 5: Challenge the Insurance Denials
Insurance carriers frequently deny chemical claims by citing 'pre-existing conditions.' They may point to your history of smoking or a childhood illness. Overcoming this requires aggressive legal representation to show that the work exposure either caused the disease or significantly aggravated an underlying condition. 

An experienced attorney at Just Work Comp Law can help you gather the specific evidence needed to bridge the gap between your workplace exposure and your medical diagnosis.
